_comment: Append-only observations: pest sightings, disease, frost damage, photos, growth notes.

observation: Supremo tomatoes: dried/collapsed upper stems, green regrowth from lower nodes. Likely cold-night transplant shock or light frost nip late May. Action: prune dead tops to healthy node, light balanced feed, re-assess in 2 weeks. One plant may be a total loss.
North pepper has snapped/stripped bamboo stake and sparse top growth. Re-stake; side shoots should take over.
Squash vine has a dried collapsed section mid-stem. Growing tip still healthy - monitor weekly.
Boysenberry foliage yellowing. Could be transplant stress or early iron chlorosis from alkaline soil. If veins stay green while leaves yellow -> chelated iron.
Heavy grassy volunteer/weed pressure in B3 and parts of B5 (likely from compost or straw). Weeding pass needed this week while small, esp. around recovering tomatoes.
Dense wall-to-wall seedling carpet. Pending: crop (thin to 5cm) vs weed flush (hoe now).
Potatoes at full canopy - hill with 10-15cm soil/straw now, repeat in ~3 weeks.

Photo review: carpet is multi-species (reddish-stem lance-leaf seedlings, likely smartweed/lady's thumb, plus mixed round-leaf cotyledons), no row pattern. Verdict: compost weed flush. Action: shallow hoe now; spare scattered beet/chard and dill-like crop seedlings.
Photo: full healthy canopy, no pest/disease visible. Ready for first hilling.
Photo: one Supremo stem fully dried, no live leaves - prune to lowest green node or remove. Second Supremo has good green regrowth from lower nodes - keep.
Photo: crown/base section dried but vine tip turgid and growing. Leave undisturbed, monitor weekly.
Photo: two unidentified woody resprouts (goji/haskap candidates) alive with fresh basal growth; one had roots partially exposed - firm soil back over root zone.
Photo: all three peppers alive; middle plant strongest. North plant's bamboo stake confirmed snapped - re-stake.

Photo: cherry leaves pale yellow-green. Watch 2 weeks - if interveinal chlorosis develops (green veins, yellow blades), treat with chelated iron like boysenberry. Blackberry shows mild older-leaf yellowing consistent with transplant stress; strawberries vigorous.

Photo: both figs leafing out from low buds after winter dieback - viable, will rebuild. Grape healthy with new growth, staked. Blueberry healthy (reddish new leaves normal). Pots rain-wet - no water needed today.
